sentences.chinese: 卷六十七·楊胡朱梅云傳第三十七
sentences.literal: Volume 67: Biographies of Yang, Hu, Zhu, Mei, and Yun—the thirty-seventh.
sentences.idiomatic: Volume 67: Biographies of Yang, Hu, Zhu, Mei, and Yun—the thirty-seventh series.

sentences.literal: Yang Wangsun.
sentences.idiomatic: Yang Wangsun

sentences.literal: Yang Wangsun was a man of Emperor Xiaowu's time.
sentences.idiomatic: Yang Wangsun lived under Emperor Wu.

sentences.chinese: 學黃老之術，家業千金，厚自奉養生，亡所不致。
sentences.literal: He studied the arts of Huang and Lao; his household estate was worth a thousand in gold; he richly provided for himself in nurturing life—nothing was left untried.
sentences.idiomatic: He studied Huang-Lao, commanded a fortune in gold, and pursued longevity with every luxury money could buy.

sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Zhi means 'to reach.'"〉
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu glosses: here 致 means "to the utmost."

sentences.chinese: 〉及病且終，先令其子，〈師古曰：「先令，爲遺令。」
sentences.literal: 〉When his illness was nearing its end, he first gave orders to his son. 〈Yan's note: "First orders means a testamentary instruction."〉
sentences.idiomatic: As death neared he issued final instructions to his son—what commentators call a "first command," meaning a deathbed will.

sentences.chinese: 〉曰：「吾欲臝葬，以反吾真，〈師古曰：「臝者，不爲衣衾棺槨者也。
sentences.literal: 〉He said, "I wish to be buried naked, to return to my authenticity. 〈Yan's note: "Naked burial means without grave-clothes, inner or outer coffin."
sentences.idiomatic: He said, "I want a naked burial—to go back to what is real. Yan Shigu: naked burial omits shroud, inner coffin, and outer shell."

sentences.literal: Fan means 'return.'
sentences.idiomatic: "Return" means to go back.

sentences.literal: The authentic is the way of spontaneity.
sentences.idiomatic: The real is nature's own course.

sentences.literal: Naked is read lang-guo fan."
sentences.idiomatic: The character 臝 is read like 裸 (stripped bare)."

sentences.literal: 〉You must not alter my intent.
sentences.idiomatic: Do not change my mind.

sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Yi means 'to change.'"〉
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: 易 here means "alter."

sentences.chinese: 〉死則爲布囊盛尸，入地七尺，旣下，從足引脫其囊，以身親土。」
sentences.literal: 〉When I die, wrap the corpse in a cloth bag, put it in the earth seven feet deep; once it is down, pull the bag off from the feet and let the body touch the soil directly."
sentences.idiomatic: When I die, sew me in a sack, lower me seven feet, then strip the sack upward from my feet until my flesh meets native earth."

sentences.chinese: 其子欲默而不從，重廢父命，〈師古曰：「重，難也。」
sentences.literal: His son wished to be silent and not obey, but found it hard to set aside his father's command. 〈Yan's note: "Zhong means 'difficult.'"〉
sentences.idiomatic: The son wanted to refuse yet shrank from disobeying a father—zhong meaning "a hard choice."

sentences.chinese: 〉欲從之，心又不忍，迺往見王孫友人祁侯。
sentences.literal: He wished to obey, yet his heart could not bear it; thereupon he went to see Wangsun's friend Marquis of Qi.
sentences.idiomatic: If he obeyed, his heart rebelled; so he went to Wangsun's friend, the Marquis of Qi.

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「祁侯繒賀之孫承嗣者，名它。」〉
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Marquis of Qi is the heir who continued the line of Zeng He, named Ta."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: the Marquis of Qi was Zeng He's grandson-heir, named Ta.

sentences.chinese: 祁侯與王孫書曰：「王孫苦疾，僕迫從上祠雍，未得詣前。
sentences.literal: The Marquis of Qi wrote to Wangsun: "Wangsun, you suffer gravely in illness; your servant was pressed to follow the Sovereign to sacrifice at Yong and could not come to your bedside.
sentences.idiomatic: Marquis Qi wrote: "Friend, your illness is cruel; I have been tied to the emperor's Yong sacrifice and could not visit you."

sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Yi means 'to arrive.'
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: 诣 is "to reach."

sentences.literal: Arriving before you means coming to see you."〉
sentences.idiomatic: To come before you is to pay a visit."

sentences.chinese: 〉願存精神，省思慮，進醫藥，厚自持。
sentences.literal: I hope you will preserve your spirit, reduce anxious thought, take medicine, and strongly sustain yourself.
sentences.idiomatic: Guard your strength, worry less, take physic, and be kind to yourself.

sentences.chinese: 竊聞王孫先令臝葬，令死者亡知則已，若其有知，是戮尸地下，將臝見先人，竊爲王孫不取也。
sentences.literal: I have privately heard of your testament ordering naked burial: if the dead have no awareness, then it ends there; if they have awareness, this is exposing the corpse to mutilation underground; you would meet your forebears naked—I privately think this unworthy for you.
sentences.idiomatic: Your naked-burial will: if the dead feel nothing, very well; if they do, you invite the shame of a corpse abused in the dark and would greet ancestors stripped—surely that cannot be your wish.

sentences.chinese: 且孝經曰『爲之棺椁衣衾』，是亦聖人之遺制，何必區區獨守所聞？
sentences.literal: Moreover the Classic of Filial Piety says 'for them make inner and outer coffin and grave-clothes'—that too is the surviving regulation of the sages; why must you narrowly cling alone to what you have heard?
sentences.idiomatic: The Classic of Filial Piety still commands coffin, shell, and winding-sheet—the sages' own rule. Why hug one eccentric opinion?

sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Ququ means petty intent."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: 区区 is narrow-minded fussing.

sentences.literal: I hope you will examine this."
sentences.idiomatic: Think again, I beg you."

sentences.chinese: 王孫報曰：「蓋聞古之聖王，緣人情不忍其親，故爲制禮，今則越之，〈師古曰：「言踰禮而厚葬也。」
sentences.literal: Wangsun replied: "I have heard that the sage kings of antiquity, following human feeling and unable to bear [separation from] their kin, therefore instituted ritual; now people leap over it. 〈Yan's note: "It means they exceed ritual with lavish burial."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Wangsun answered: "The ancient kings framed rites from human pity for the dead; today men vault past those bounds into wasteful tombs. Yan Shigu notes: they overspend the rites."

sentences.literal: Therefore I use naked burial—I will thereby correct the age.
sentences.idiomatic: I choose naked burial to jerk the age back toward sanity.

sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "To straighten the crooked is jiao."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: 矫 is to set a bent thing straight.

sentences.chinese: 〉夫厚葬誠亡益於死者，而俗人競以相高，靡財單幣，腐之地下。
sentences.literal: Now lavish burial truly brings no benefit to the dead, yet vulgar men vie to outdo one another in it, scattering wealth and exhausting treasure to rot it underground.
sentences.idiomatic: Rich tombs help the corpse not at all, yet the world competes in piling treasure underground until fortunes dissolve.

sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Mi means 'to scatter.'
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: 靡 is "squander."

sentences.literal: Dan means 'to exhaust.'"〉
sentences.idiomatic: Dan means "drain dry."

sentences.chinese: 〉或迺今日入而明日發，〈師古曰：「言見發掘也。」
sentences.literal: Some are interred today and exhumed tomorrow. 〈Yan's note: "It means they are dug up by robbers."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Some are sealed tonight and rifled tomorrow—Yan Shigu: opened by thieves.

sentences.literal: How is this different from exposing bones in the open field!
sentences.idiomatic: That is no better than leaving carrion on the plain.

sentences.chinese: 且夫死者，終生之化，而物之歸者也。
sentences.literal: Moreover the dead are the transformation that ends life and the return to which things go.
sentences.idiomatic: The dead are life's last change, the homecoming all matter makes.

sentences.chinese: 歸者得至，化者得變，是物各反其真也。
sentences.literal: What returns can reach its end; what transforms can change—thus each thing turns back to its authenticity.
sentences.idiomatic: Return finishes the journey; change sheds the old—each creature goes home to its own nature.

sentences.chinese: 反真冥冥，亡形亡聲，迺合道情。
sentences.literal: Returning to authenticity is dark and silent, without form or sound—then it matches the disposition of the Way.
sentences.idiomatic: In that dark return, shape and voice gone, the man at last fits the Way.

sentences.chinese: 夫飾外以華衆，厚葬以鬲真，〈師古曰：「鬲與隔同。
sentences.literal: To adorn the outside to dazzle the crowd, to bury thickly and thereby block the authentic—〈Yan's note: "Ge is the same as ge 'to block.'
sentences.idiomatic: Gaudy display for the living, thick coffins for the dead—both wall off the real. Yan Shigu: 鬲 reads like 隔."

sentences.literal: What follows is all of this kind."〉
sentences.idiomatic: The glosses that follow run on the same principle."

sentences.chinese: 〉使歸者不得至，化者不得變，是使物各失其所也。
sentences.literal: It makes what should return unable to reach its end, what should transform unable to change—thus each thing loses its proper station.
sentences.idiomatic: You trap the traveler short of the inn and freeze the chrysalis in the shell—every creature misses its appointed end.

sentences.chinese: 且吾聞之，精神者天之有也，形骸者地之有也。
sentences.literal: Moreover I have heard that the vital spirit belongs to what Heaven has, the bodily frame to what Earth has.
sentences.idiomatic: The hun soul is heaven's loan; the bones are earth's clay.

sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "The Wenzi calls heaven's breath the hun.
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu cites the Wenzi: celestial breath is the ethereal soul."

sentences.chinese: 延陵季子云『骨肉下歸於土』，是以云然。」
sentences.literal: Jizha of Yanling said, 'Flesh and bone sink down to earth'—therefore it is said so."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Jizha of Yanling said, "Flesh and bone return to mud"—the same teaching."

sentences.chinese: 〉精神離形，各歸其真，故謂之鬼，鬼之爲言歸也。
sentences.literal: The spirit leaves the form; each returns to its authenticity—therefore it is called a gui; speaking of gui means 'return.'
sentences.idiomatic: Spirit and carcass part, each to its element—hence we call the dead gui, "those who have gone home."

sentences.chinese: 其尸塊然獨處，〈師古曰：「塊音口對反。」
sentences.literal: The corpse sits there lumpish and alone. 〈Yan's note: "Kuai is read kou-dui fan."〉
sentences.idiomatic: The husk sits lumpish, alone. Yan Shigu gives the fanqie for 塊.

sentences.literal: How could it have awareness?
sentences.idiomatic: What mind is left in meat?

sentences.chinese: 裹以幣帛，鬲以棺椁，支體絡束，口含玉石，欲化不得，鬱爲枯腊，千載之後，棺椁朽腐，迺得歸土，就其真宅。
sentences.literal: Wrapped in silks, blocked by inner and outer coffins, limbs trussed, mouth filled with jade and stone—it cannot transform but stagnates into dry flesh; after a thousand years, when coffin shells rot, only then can it return to earth and reach its true dwelling.
sentences.idiomatic: Swaddle it in brocade, box it in double caskets, bind the limbs, plug the mouth with jade—you forbid decay until it mummifies; only when wood rots a thousand years later does dust rejoin dust.

sentences.literal: From this speaking, what use is long lodging as a guest!
sentences.idiomatic: Why prolong a guest lease on nothing?

sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "It means do not long remain a guest.
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: do not overstay as a traveler in the world."

sentences.literal: You is read the same as you."〉
sentences.idiomatic: You (由) is the same graph as 繇 here."

sentences.chinese: 〉昔帝堯之葬也，窾木爲匵，葛藟爲緘，〈服虔曰：「窾音款。
sentences.literal: In antiquity, when Emperor Yao was buried, they hollowed wood for a chest and used creeper of dolichos as ties. 〈Fu Qian's note: "Kuan is read kuan; kuan means 'hollow'; hollow wood for a chest."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Emperor Yao's grave was a hollowed log for coffin, wild grapevine for straps—Fu Qian: 窾 is hollowed trunk.

sentences.literal: Kuan means empty; empty wood for a chest."
sentences.idiomatic: Empty timber formed the shell."

sentences.literal: Yan's note: "Du is the same character as du 'chest.'
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: 匵 is the 櫝 chest.

sentences.literal: Du means a small coffin.
sentences.idiomatic: A small inner box.

sentences.literal: Lei means creeper of dolichos.
sentences.idiomatic: Lei is dolichos vine.

sentences.chinese: 一曰，藟亦草名，葛之類也。
sentences.literal: One opinion says lei is also a plant name, a kind of dolichos.
sentences.idiomatic: Another gloss: lei names a vine like kudzu.

sentences.literal: Jian means to bind.
sentences.idiomatic: Jian means lash tight.

sentences.literal: Lei is read li-shui fan.
sentences.idiomatic: Fanqie for 藟.

sentences.literal: Jian is read gong-xian fan.""
sentences.idiomatic: Fanqie for 緘."

sentences.chinese: 〉其穿下不亂泉，〈師古曰：「亂，絕也。」
sentences.literal: 〉The shaft did not cut off the spring below, 〈Yan's note: "Luan means 'to cut off.'"〉
sentences.idiomatic: The pit did not pierce the aquifer below—Yan Shigu: 乱 is "cut off" here.

sentences.literal: Above it did not vent stench to the sky.
sentences.idiomatic: Nor did stench seep upward to offend heaven.

sentences.chinese: 故聖王生易尚，死易葬也。
sentences.literal: Therefore the sage kings found life easy to honor and death easy to bury.
sentences.idiomatic: Thus the sage kings lived simply and buried simply.

sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Shang means 'to esteem.'
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: 尚 is "honor."

sentences.literal: It means both life and death were frugal."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Both ends of life stayed spare."

sentences.chinese: 〉不加功於亡用，不損財於亡謂。
sentences.literal: They did not add labor to the useless, did not waste wealth on meaningless display.
sentences.idiomatic: They wasted no labor on the pointless, no coin on empty show.

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「謂者，名稱也，亦指趣也。」
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Wei means name, also meaning aim or tendency."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: 谓 is "title" or "pretext."

sentences.chinese: 〉今費財厚葬，留歸鬲至，死者不知，生者不得，是謂重惑。
sentences.literal: Now to squander wealth on thick burial, to detain the return and block arrival—the dead know nothing of it, the living gain nothing from it: this is called double delusion.
sentences.idiomatic: Today men bankrupt kin for tombs that neither comfort the dead nor enrich the living—twice deluded.

sentences.literal: Alas!
sentences.idiomatic: Alas!

sentences.literal: I will not do it."
sentences.idiomatic: I will not do it."

sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Yu is read wu.
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: 於 reads wū."

sentences.literal: Xi is read hu."〉
sentences.idiomatic: 戲 reads like 呼 (an exclamation)."

sentences.literal: The Marquis of Qi said, "Excellent."
sentences.idiomatic: Marquis Qi said, "Well argued."

sentences.literal: Thereupon he was buried naked.
sentences.idiomatic: They buried him as he asked—stripped to earth.

sentences.literal: Hu Jian, courtesy name Zimeng, was a man of Hedong.
sentences.idiomatic: Hu Jian, courtesy Zimeng, came from Hedong.

sentences.chinese: 孝武天漢中，守軍正丞，〈師古曰：「南北軍各有正，正又置丞，而建未得真官，兼守之。」
sentences.literal: In Emperor Wu's Tianhan era he acted as assistant to the army's chief rectifier. 〈Yan's note: "North and south armies each had a chief rectifier, who again had an assistant; Jian had not yet obtained substantive office but was holding the post concurrently."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Under Wu's Tianhan reign he served as acting deputy to the chief army judge—Yan Shigu: each host had a rectifier and deputy; Jian held the post without full rank.

sentences.chinese: 〉貧亡車馬，常步與走卒起居，所以尉薦走卒，甚得其心。
sentences.literal: He was too poor for carriage or horse and always walked, sharing rise and rest with the runners, whereby he comforted and advanced the runners—he won their hearts extremely.
sentences.idiomatic: Too poor for a cart, he marched with the camp runners, sharing their bed and board until they loved him.

sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Wei means to soothe from above.
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: 尉 is to reassure from above."

sentences.literal: Jian means to raise on the roster."〉
sentences.idiomatic: 荐 is to enroll and promote."

sentences.chinese: 〉時監軍御史爲姦，穿北軍壘垣以爲賈區，〈師古曰：「坐賣曰賈，爲賣物之區也。
sentences.literal: At that time the supervising army Imperial Clerk did wrong, breaching the north army's rampart to make a market stall. 〈Yan's note: "Sitting sale is gu; it means a place for selling goods.
sentences.idiomatic: The supervising censor broke the camp palisade to open a shop—Yan Shigu: 贾 is a seated market stall."

sentences.chinese: 區者，小室之名，若今小庵屋之類耳。
sentences.literal: Qu means the name of a small chamber, like today's little hut.
sentences.idiomatic: A 区 is a booth, like a guard shack.

sentences.chinese: 故衞士之屋謂之區廬，宿衞宮外士稱爲區士也。
sentences.literal: Therefore the guards' quarters are called qu-lu, and the guards who lodge outside the palace are called qu-shi.
sentences.idiomatic: Hence guard sheds are 区庐 and exterior sentries 区士.

sentences.literal: Gu is read gu.
sentences.idiomatic: 贾 is read gǔ.

sentences.literal: What follows below is the same."〉
sentences.idiomatic: The same reading applies below."

sentences.chinese: 〉建欲誅之，迺約其走卒曰：〈師古曰：「約，束也。」
sentences.literal: Jian wished to execute him; thereupon he bound his runners with agreement. 〈Yan's note: "Yue means 'to bind.'"〉
sentences.idiomatic: Jian resolved to kill him and bound his runners with an oath—Yan Shigu: 约 is "bind" to a pledge.

sentences.chinese: 〉「我欲與公有所誅，吾言取之則取，斬之則斬。」
sentences.literal: "I wish with you gentlemen to execute someone: when I say 'take him,' take; when I say 'strike him off,' strike him off."
sentences.idiomatic: "We are going to kill a man: when I say take, you seize; when I say strike, you strike."

sentences.chinese: 於是當選士馬日，監御史與護軍諸校列坐堂皇上，〈師古曰：「校者，軍之諸部校也。
sentences.literal: Therefore on the day when picked troops and horses were reviewed, the supervising clerk and the protectors' various colonels sat in rows in the open hall. 〈Yan's note: "Colonel means the army's various colonels' sections.
sentences.idiomatic: On muster day the censor and the colonels sat in the roofless hall—Yan Shigu: 校 are the camp sections."

sentences.literal: A chamber without four walls is called huang."〉
sentences.idiomatic: 皇 is an open hall without four walls."

sentences.chinese: 〉建從走卒趨至堂皇下拜謁，因上堂皇，走卒皆上，建指監御史曰：「取彼。」
sentences.literal: Jian came from the runners at a run to the foot of the hall, bowed in audience, then ascended the hall; the runners all ascended; Jian pointed at the supervising clerk and said, "Take that man."
sentences.idiomatic: Jian rushed up with the runners, bowed, mounted the platform, and pointed: "Seize him."

sentences.literal: The runners came forward and dragged him down from the hall.
sentences.idiomatic: They dragged the censor from the dais.

sentences.literal: Jian said, "Strike him off."
sentences.idiomatic: "Behead him."

sentences.literal: Thereupon they beheaded the clerk.
sentences.idiomatic: The blade fell.

sentences.chinese: 護軍諸校皆愕驚，不知所以。
sentences.literal: The protectors' colonels were all stunned and alarmed, not knowing why.
sentences.idiomatic: The colonels sat frozen, not grasping what had happened.

sentences.chinese: 建亦已有成奏在其懷中，遂上奏曰：「臣聞軍法，立武以威衆，誅惡以禁邪。
sentences.literal: Jian already had a completed memorial in his bosom; thereupon he submitted a memorial saying, "Your servant has heard that in military law one establishes martial awesomeness to overawe the masses and punishes evil to forbid deviance.
sentences.idiomatic: Jian drew from his breast a finished memorial: "The code says awe checks the ranks and punishment bars crime."

sentences.chinese: 今監御史公穿軍垣以求賈利，〈師古曰：「公謂顯然爲之。」
sentences.literal: Now the supervising clerk has publicly bored through the army wall to seek merchant profit. 〈Yan's note: "Gong means he did it openly."〉
sentences.idiomatic: This censor openly breached the camp wall for trade—Yan Shigu: 公 means "openly."

sentences.chinese: 〉私買賣以與士巿，不立剛毅之心，勇猛之節，亡以帥先士大夫，尤失理不公。
sentences.literal: Privately buying and selling to market with the soldiers—he does not set up a heart of firm sternness or a quality of fierce courage; he has no means to lead and take the lead of great officers and gentlemen—this is especially losing principle and lacking fairness.
sentences.idiomatic: He peddled inside the lines, showed no stern courage, and could not lead officers—gross injustice by statute.

sentences.literal: If judged by clerical deliberation, it would not reach heavy penalty.
sentences.idiomatic: A civil tribunal might call it a light offense.

sentences.chinese: 黃帝李法曰：〈蘇林曰：「獄官名也。
sentences.literal: The Yellow Emperor's Li Code says: 〈Su Lin's note: "It is the name of a prison official.
sentences.idiomatic: The Yellow Emperor's Li canons—Su Lin: Li was a judicial title."

sentences.literal: The Treatise on Astronomy says 'left horn Li, right horn general.'"〉
sentences.idiomatic: The monograph on astronomy pairs the left horn with Li."

sentences.literal: Meng Kang's note: "It is the method in military books.""
sentences.idiomatic: Meng Kang: a military-law formula."

sentences.chinese: 師古曰：「李者，法官之號也，緫主征伐刑戮之事也，故稱其書曰李法。
sentences.literal: Yan's note: "Li is the title of a judge; he overall masters punitive expeditions and executions; therefore their book is called the Li Code.
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: Li judges war and punishment, hence the Li Code—Su Lin's gloss is closest."

sentences.literal: Su's explanation is near the mark."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Su Lin had the sense of it."

sentences.chinese: 〉『壁壘已定，穿窬不繇路，是謂姦人，姦人者殺。』
sentences.literal: 'When ramparts and palisades are already fixed, to bore a thief-hole not by the road—this is called a traitor; traitors are killed.'"
sentences.idiomatic: "Once walls stand, tunneling off the line is treason—treason earns death."
